enrichment.short_description: Close is an all-in-one CRM with built-in calling, email, and SMS built for fast-moving inside sales teams.
enrichment.starting_price: From approximately $19/user/month (billed annually) on the Startup plan
enrichment.headquarters: San Francisco, California, USA (fully remote team)

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Close, originally launched as Close.io in 2013, is a sales CRM built around the idea that reps shouldn't need a stack of separate tools to prospect, call, email, and close deals. Instead of bolting a dialer or email tool onto a traditional CRM, Close bakes calling, SMS, and email directly into the pipeline, so outreach and deal tracking happen in one place.

The company has operated as a fully remote, distributed team since its early days, which shaped a product philosophy centered on speed, simplicity, and getting sales reps productive with minimal setup. Close markets itself less as a system of record for enterprise operations and more as a working tool that inside sales teams use dozens of times a day.

Over the years Close has added workflow automation, predictive and power dialers, email sequences, and reporting, but it has generally resisted becoming a sprawling platform. The result is a CRM that is opinionated about supporting high-velocity, call-and-email-heavy sales motions rather than trying to be everything to every type of go-to-market team.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Close Is For
paragraphs: Close is best suited to small and mid-sized B2B companies, especially startups and scale-ups, where a lean sales team is doing a high volume of outbound calling and emailing. Inside sales reps, SDRs, and BDRs who spend most of their day on the phone benefit directly from built-in calling features that eliminate the need for a separate dialer subscription.

It also fits sales leaders who want a CRM that can be configured and adopted quickly without a lengthy implementation project or a dedicated CRM administrator. Because Close is cloud-based and doesn't require heavy IT involvement, remote and distributed sales teams often find it straightforward to roll out.

Founders and small sales teams moving off spreadsheets, or teams that previously duct-taped together a CRM plus a separate calling app, are a common fit, since Close consolidates those workflows into a single subscription and interface.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Large enterprises with complex org structures, multi-department approval workflows, or deep custom object requirements will likely find Close's data model and configurability more limited than platforms like Salesforce. Teams that need extensive role-based permission hierarchies or industry-specific compliance customization may hit ceilings.

Companies that need robust marketing automation, customer support ticketing, or a broad third-party app marketplace as part of the same platform should look elsewhere, since Close is focused specifically on the sales pipeline rather than being a full revenue-operations suite.

Organizations already standardized on a larger CRM ecosystem, or those that rely heavily on a vast library of pre-built integrations and partner apps, may find Close's integration catalog narrower by comparison, even though it does connect with common tools like Zapier and Google Workspace.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: Close's signature feature is its built-in calling, including a Power Dialer and Predictive Dialer for working through call lists quickly, plus native SMS messaging, all logged automatically against the relevant contact and deal. Email is similarly built in, supporting two-way sync with existing inboxes and automated, multi-step email sequences for outreach and follow-up.

Pipeline management gives reps and managers a visual view of deals by stage, and Workflows lets teams automate repetitive follow-up tasks and reminders. Reporting dashboards surface call activity, email performance, and pipeline metrics so managers can track rep productivity without exporting data elsewhere.

Custom fields and custom activities allow teams to adapt the CRM to their own sales process, and an open API plus integrations (such as Zapier, Zoom, and Calendly) let Close connect into a broader tech stack when needed.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Close Experience
paragraphs: Day to day, using Close feels closer to using a communications tool than a traditional database-style CRM. The interface is built around the list of leads or deals a rep is working through right now, with calling, texting, and emailing available directly from that view rather than in a separate app.

New users generally report a shorter ramp-up time than with heavier enterprise CRMs, since there are fewer configuration screens and less need for an administrator to model the system before reps can start using it. That simplicity is part of the trade-off: teams wanting extensive customization or a large ecosystem of add-ons will find Close comparatively streamlined.

Overall, the experience rewards teams whose sales process centers on high-frequency phone and email touches, giving reps a single screen to move quickly from one conversation to the next while keeping activity history automatically attached to each contact and deal.

features.title: Built-in Calling
features.description: Make and receive calls directly inside the CRM, with call recording and automatic activity logging against each contact.

features.title: Power & Predictive Dialer
features.description: Work through call lists faster with dialers that automatically queue up the next call, reducing manual dialing time.

features.title: Native SMS
features.description: Send and receive text messages from within Close, logged alongside calls and emails on the contact timeline.

features.title: Two-Way Email Sync & Sequences
features.description: Sync existing email inboxes and set up automated, multi-step email sequences for outreach and follow-up.

features.title: Visual Pipeline Management
features.description: Track deals through customizable pipeline stages with a drag-and-drop view for reps and managers.

features.title: Workflow Automation
features.description: Automate follow-up tasks, reminders, and multi-channel outreach sequences triggered by lead or deal activity.

features.title: Reporting & Analytics
features.description: Dashboards and reports covering call activity, email performance, and sales pipeline metrics for coaching and forecasting.

features.title: Custom Fields & Activities
features.description: Adapt the data model with custom fields and activity types to match a team's specific sales process.

features.title: Open API
features.description: A documented API for building custom integrations or syncing data with other business systems.

features.title: Third-Party Integrations
features.description: Connects with tools like Zapier, Google Workspace, Zoom, and Calendly to extend the core CRM.

pros: Calling, SMS, and email are all built in, reducing the need for separate outreach tools
Fast setup and a relatively short learning curve compared to enterprise CRMs
Power and Predictive dialers help high-volume outbound teams work through leads efficiently
Straightforward pipeline view keeps deal tracking and communication in one screen
Workflow automation reduces manual follow-up work for reps
cons: Less configurable than large enterprise CRMs for complex organizational structures
No built-in marketing automation or customer support ticketing functionality
Smaller third-party app marketplace compared to major CRM platforms
Calling minutes and add-ons can increase costs depending on usage
May feel limited for teams that need deep customization of objects and permissions

faqs.question: What is Close used for?
faqs.answer: Close is a sales CRM used by inside sales teams to manage leads and deals while calling, texting, and emailing prospects from within a single platform.

faqs.question: Does Close have a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, Close offers a free trial period so teams can test the CRM, calling, and email features before subscribing to a paid plan.

faqs.question: Is there a free plan for Close?
faqs.answer: Close does not offer a permanent free plan; it is a paid subscription product with tiered pricing based on features and usage.

faqs.question: Does Close have mobile apps?
faqs.answer: Close offers mobile apps for iOS and Android so reps can call, text, and manage deals from a phone or tablet.

faqs.question: Can Close integrate with other tools?
faqs.answer: Yes, Close supports integrations such as Zapier, Google Workspace, Zoom, and Calendly, along with an open API for custom connections.

use_cases.title: Outbound Sales Prospecting
use_cases.description: SDRs use Close's Power and Predictive dialers to work through call lists and log every touchpoint automatically.

use_cases.title: Founder-Led Sales
use_cases.description: Early-stage founders manage their own pipeline and outreach without needing a dedicated sales operations setup.
use_cases.audience: Startup founders and small sales teams

use_cases.title: Remote Sales Team Management
use_cases.description: Sales managers track call and email activity across a distributed team using built-in reporting dashboards.

use_cases.title: Deal Pipeline Tracking
use_cases.description: Sales teams move opportunities through customizable pipeline stages while keeping communication history attached to each deal.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: See pricing, features, pros and cons for Close, the CRM with built-in calling and email for inside sales teams.

enrichment.short_description: Global CDN, DNS, and edge-compute platform that speeds up, protects, and scales websites, apps, and APIs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Cloudflare is a global network and edge-computing platform best known for making websites and applications faster, more reliable, and more secure. Founded in 2009 and headquartered in San Francisco, the company operates one of the largest content delivery networks (CDNs) in the world, routing traffic through data centers spread across dozens of countries so that content is served from a location physically close to the end user.

Beyond the CDN, Cloudflare's core platform bundles authoritative DNS hosting, automatic DDoS mitigation, a web application firewall, SSL/TLS certificate management, and image and traffic optimization into a single dashboard. On top of that infrastructure sits Cloudflare's developer platform: Workers, a serverless JavaScript/WebAssembly runtime that executes code at the network edge, and Pages, a Git-connected static site and full-stack hosting product, along with supporting services like Workers KV, Durable Objects, and R2 object storage.

Cloudflare is publicly traded on the NYSE (ticker: NET) and is widely used by everyone from individual bloggers to large enterprises. This listing focuses on Cloudflare's core CDN, DNS, and edge-compute platform; Cloudflare Zero Trust (the company's separate network security and remote access suite) is covered in its own listing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Cloudflare Is For
paragraphs: Cloudflare is a strong fit for website owners, developers, and IT teams who need faster page loads, protection against downtime, and simplified DNS management without standing up their own infrastructure. Its free tier makes it accessible to solo developers, small businesses, and hobby projects that just want a CDN, basic DDoS protection, and free SSL in front of an existing site.

It also serves developers building on the edge: teams using Workers and Pages to ship serverless APIs, Jamstack sites, and full-stack applications that need low-latency execution close to users, without managing servers. Larger organizations and enterprises turn to Cloudflare's Business and Enterprise plans for advanced WAF rules, bot management, load balancing, and SLA-backed performance and uptime guarantees across high-traffic or business-critical properties.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Cloudflare's core platform assumes a website, application, or API that can be routed through Cloudflare's network — it isn't the right tool for teams looking for a full application hosting stack with managed databases, backend frameworks, or CI/CD out of the box, since Workers and Pages are intentionally lightweight and edge-focused rather than general-purpose cloud hosting.

Organizations that need deep enterprise remote-access, private network, and Zero Trust security controls should look at Cloudflare's dedicated Zero Trust product rather than this core CDN/edge listing. Teams with very simple static sites and no need for DNS-level control, and those uncomfortable routing DNS or traffic through a third-party network, may also find the setup and proxy model more than they need.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: At its core, Cloudflare provides a global CDN that caches and serves content from edge locations worldwide, paired with authoritative DNS hosting known for fast query resolution. Security features include automatic Layer 3/4 and Layer 7 DDoS mitigation, a configurable web application firewall (WAF), bot management, rate limiting, and free automated SSL/TLS certificate issuance and renewal.

On the developer side, Cloudflare Workers lets teams deploy JavaScript, TypeScript, Rust, or WebAssembly code to run at the edge with very low cold-start latency, while Cloudflare Pages provides Git-integrated deployment for static sites and full-stack frameworks. Supporting services such as Workers KV (key-value storage), Durable Objects (stateful coordination), R2 (S3-compatible object storage without egress fees), and D1 (serverless SQL database) round out the platform for building edge-native applications.

Additional capabilities include image and video optimization, load balancing and traffic steering across origins, analytics and real-time traffic insights, and Argo Smart Routing for reducing latency across the public internet. All of these are managed through a unified dashboard and API, with infrastructure-as-code support via Terraform and Wrangler (Cloudflare's CLI for Workers).

long_form_blocks.heading: The Cloudflare Experience
paragraphs: Getting started with Cloudflare typically means pointing a domain's nameservers to Cloudflare, after which the CDN, DNS, and security features activate with sensible defaults — a process most users can complete in minutes even without a networking background. The dashboard organizes settings by category (DNS, SSL/TLS, Security, Speed, Caching, Workers) and surfaces real-time analytics on traffic, threats blocked, and cache performance.

Developers working with Workers and Pages generally describe the experience as fast to iterate in, thanks to near-instant global deployments and a generous free tier for testing ideas before scaling up. The tradeoff some teams note is that the breadth of the product surface — CDN, security, DNS, and a full serverless platform — means the dashboard and documentation can take time to fully navigate, and advanced configuration (custom WAF rules, complex Workers logic) benefits from some technical familiarity.

Overall, Cloudflare is regarded as a reliable, well-documented platform with an active developer community, frequent product updates, and a reputation for transparency during major internet outages and security incidents, which has helped build long-term trust with both individual developers and enterprise customers.

features.title: Global CDN
features.description: Caches and delivers content from edge locations around the world to reduce latency and speed up page loads.

features.title: Authoritative DNS
features.description: Fast, reliable DNS hosting with an easy-to-use management interface and API.

features.title: DDoS Protection
features.description: Automatic mitigation of Layer 3, 4, and 7 distributed denial-of-service attacks included on every plan.

features.title: Web Application Firewall (WAF)
features.description: Configurable rule sets to block common web exploits, malicious bots, and application-layer attacks.

features.title: Free SSL/TLS Certificates
features.description: Automated certificate issuance and renewal to encrypt traffic between visitors and origin servers.

features.title: Cloudflare Workers
features.description: A serverless runtime for executing JavaScript, TypeScript, Rust, and WebAssembly code at the network edge.

features.title: Cloudflare Pages
features.description: Git-connected hosting for static sites and full-stack applications with automatic deployments.

features.title: R2 Object Storage
features.description: S3-compatible object storage designed to avoid the egress fees typical of traditional cloud storage.

features.title: Load Balancing
features.description: Distributes traffic across multiple origin servers and regions to improve uptime and performance.

features.title: Analytics Dashboard
features.description: Real-time visibility into traffic, cache performance, and security threats blocked at the edge.

pros: Generous free tier covering CDN, DNS, DDoS protection, and SSL
Massive global network that reduces latency for end users worldwide
Strong, well-documented developer platform (Workers, Pages, R2)
Reputation for reliability and transparency, including during major internet incidents
Simple setup for basic use cases via nameserver-based onboarding
cons: Breadth of the product surface can feel overwhelming for newcomers
Advanced WAF and Workers configuration benefits from technical/networking knowledge
Routing traffic through a third-party proxy is a tradeoff some site owners are cautious about
Not a full application hosting stack for complex, stateful backend systems
Higher-tier plans (Business, Enterprise) can be a significant cost step up from the free tier

faqs.question: Is Cloudflare free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cloudflare offers a free plan that includes CDN, DNS hosting, basic DDoS protection, and free SSL certificates. Paid Pro, Business, and Enterprise plans add more advanced security, performance, and support features.

faqs.question: What is Cloudflare Workers?
faqs.answer: Workers is Cloudflare's serverless compute platform that lets developers run JavaScript, TypeScript, Rust, and WebAssembly code directly on Cloudflare's edge network, close to end users, without managing servers.

faqs.question: Does Cloudflare replace my web host?
faqs.answer: Not entirely for traditional sites — Cloudflare sits in front of your existing host as a CDN and security layer. However, Cloudflare Pages can fully host static sites and certain full-stack applications directly.

faqs.question: Is Cloudflare the same as Cloudflare Zero Trust?
faqs.answer: No. This listing covers Cloudflare's core CDN, DNS, and edge-compute platform. Cloudflare Zero Trust is a separate product suite focused on network security and secure remote access, and it is listed separately.

faqs.question: Do I need to change my domain's nameservers to use Cloudflare?
faqs.answer: For the core CDN and DNS features, yes — most setups involve pointing your domain's nameservers to Cloudflare. Some features, like Workers or Pages, can also be used independently in certain configurations.

use_cases.title: Speeding up a business website
use_cases.description: Route a website through Cloudflare's CDN to cache static assets globally and reduce load times for visitors regardless of location.
use_cases.audience: Small business owners and marketing teams

use_cases.title: Protecting against DDoS and bot traffic
use_cases.description: Use automatic DDoS mitigation, WAF rules, and bot management to keep a site or API online during attack traffic spikes.

use_cases.title: Building serverless APIs at the edge
use_cases.description: Deploy lightweight APIs and backend logic using Cloudflare Workers, running close to users with minimal cold-start latency.

use_cases.title: Hosting Jamstack and static sites
use_cases.description: Connect a Git repository to Cloudflare Pages for automatic builds and global deployment of static or full-stack front ends.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: See Cloudflare's CDN, DNS, DDoS protection, and Workers edge-compute pricing, features, pros, cons, and FAQs before you sign up.

enrichment.short_description: Cloudflare Zero Trust replaces VPNs with identity-based access, a secure web gateway, and browser isolation on Cloudflare's global network.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Cloudflare Zero Trust is Cloudflare's security product line for controlling who and what can reach corporate applications, internal networks, and the open internet, without relying on a traditional perimeter or a legacy VPN. It applies a 'never trust, always verify' model: every connection request, whether from an employee laptop, a contractor's browser, or a server-to-server API call, is authenticated, authorized, and inspected against policy before access is granted, regardless of whether the user is on the corporate network or working remotely.

The product is built on the same global network Cloudflare uses for its CDN and DNS services, but it is a distinct suite sold and configured separately: Cloudflare Access, Gateway (secure web gateway), Tunnel, Browser Isolation, CASB, DLP, and the WARP client. These pieces share one control plane, called Cloudflare One, so a security team can set an identity- and device-aware policy once and have it enforced consistently across private applications, SaaS tools, and general internet traffic.

Cloudflare positions Zero Trust as a lighter-weight, network-native alternative to appliance-heavy SASE and ZTNA vendors such as Zscaler, Netskope, and Palo Alto Prisma Access. Because policy enforcement happens at Cloudflare's edge locations rather than in a centralized data center, the pitch is lower latency for end users and less hardware for IT to manage, and pricing starts with a genuinely usable free tier rather than a sales-gated trial.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Cloudflare Zero Trust Is For
paragraphs: Cloudflare Zero Trust is aimed at IT and security teams, typically at mid-size to large organizations, that are actively retiring site-to-site or client VPNs in favor of per-application, identity-based access. It suits organizations with a hybrid or fully remote workforce that need employees, contractors, and third-party vendors to reach internal apps and SaaS tools securely without being placed on the full corporate network.

It's also a good fit for teams that already use other Cloudflare products (DNS, CDN, WAF) and want to consolidate security tooling under a single vendor and dashboard rather than stitching together separate VPN, web filtering, and DLP products. Organizations in regulated industries that need audit trails, DNS/HTTP filtering, and data loss prevention across both managed and unmanaged devices are common adopters, as are companies pursuing a broader SASE consolidation strategy, and startups that want enterprise-grade access controls without an enterprise budget, since the free tier covers up to 50 users.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Very small teams with only a handful of employees and simple needs may find the setup effort disproportionate to the problem; a basic VPN or an off-the-shelf remote-access tool can be enough at that scale, at least until growth or compliance pressure changes the calculus.

Organizations without any in-house networking or security administration capacity should also be cautious. Zero Trust's value comes from policy design (device posture rules, identity provider integration, application-by-application access rules), and getting that wrong can either lock out legitimate users or leave gaps. Teams that want a fully turnkey, zero-configuration product, or that need the deepest CASB and DLP capabilities without the cost and negotiation of an Enterprise contract, may be better served by a specialist point solution instead. Companies with heavy investment in an existing SASE vendor's ecosystem may also find switching costs outweigh the benefits of moving.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: At its core, Cloudflare Access delivers Zero Trust Network Access (ZTNA): instead of a flat VPN tunnel into the network, users authenticate through an identity provider and are granted access to individual applications based on policy, with no inbound ports exposed. Cloudflare Gateway is the secure web gateway layer, applying DNS and HTTP/HTTPS filtering, malware scanning, and content policies to outbound traffic, while Cloudflare Tunnel lets teams expose internal resources to Access without opening firewall ports.

Beyond access and filtering, the suite adds Remote Browser Isolation, which renders risky or uncategorized web pages in an isolated cloud browser and streams only pixels to the endpoint, and CASB, which scans connected SaaS platforms like Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, and Salesforce for misconfigurations and exposed data. Data Loss Prevention scans uploads and downloads for sensitive patterns such as card numbers or custom regexes, and these controls can all be layered onto the same Gateway policy rather than requiring separate tools.

Rounding out the platform is the WARP client, a lightweight agent for Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, and Android that routes device traffic through Cloudflare's network and enforces device posture checks, plus Email Security and broad identity provider integrations (Okta, Azure AD, Google Workspace, and others) so existing SSO setups carry over directly into Zero Trust policy without rebuilding user directories.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Zero Trust Experience
paragraphs: Day-to-day administration happens through the Cloudflare One dashboard, where policies for Access, Gateway, and DLP are configured in one place rather than across separate consoles. Teams already familiar with Cloudflare's DNS or WAF dashboard will recognize the interface conventions, though building out granular per-application and per-group rules takes deliberate policy design, especially for larger organizations with many SaaS apps and user groups.

For end users, the experience is meant to be close to invisible: the WARP client runs in the background, applications behind Access present a branded login screen tied to the company's identity provider, and browser isolation or DLP actions generally happen without the user noticing anything beyond an occasional blocked-page message. Rollout typically involves connecting an identity provider, deploying WARP to managed devices, and defining Access applications incrementally rather than a single cutover.

Because policy enforcement runs on Cloudflare's distributed network rather than a centralized data center, performance for globally dispersed teams tends to hold up well compared to traditional VPN concentrators. Support and documentation quality vary by plan tier, with Enterprise customers getting dedicated support and SLAs, while free and pay-as-you-go users rely mainly on community and standard documentation.

features.title: Cloudflare Access (ZTNA)
features.description: Grants identity-based, per-application access to internal tools and web apps without exposing them to the public internet or requiring a network-wide VPN tunnel.

features.title: Cloudflare Gateway
features.description: A secure web gateway that filters DNS and HTTP/HTTPS traffic, blocks malware and phishing destinations, and enforces acceptable-use policies for outbound internet activity.

features.title: Cloudflare Tunnel
features.description: Connects internal servers and applications to Cloudflare's network through an outbound-only connector, removing the need to open inbound firewall ports.

features.title: Remote Browser Isolation
features.description: Runs risky or uncategorized web pages in an isolated cloud browser and streams only rendered pixels to the endpoint, keeping active content off the local device.

features.title: CASB
features.description: Agentless scanning of connected SaaS platforms such as Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, and Salesforce for misconfigurations, risky sharing settings, and exposed data.

features.title: Data Loss Prevention (DLP)
features.description: Inspects HTTP uploads and downloads for sensitive data patterns, including built-in and custom detection rules, to prevent leakage of regulated information.

features.title: WARP client
features.description: A lightweight device agent for Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, and Android that routes traffic through Cloudflare's network and checks device posture before granting access.

features.title: Email Security
features.description: Filters phishing, malware, and business email compromise attempts in cloud email platforms as part of the broader Zero Trust policy set.

features.title: Identity provider integrations
features.description: Connects to Okta, Azure AD, Google Workspace, and other identity providers so existing single sign-on and group structures drive Zero Trust policy directly.

features.title: Unified Cloudflare One dashboard
features.description: Centralizes policy configuration, logs, and analytics for Access, Gateway, CASB, and DLP in a single control plane instead of separate point-product consoles.

pros: Genuinely usable free plan covering up to 50 users with full ZTNA and secure web gateway functionality
Policy enforcement runs on Cloudflare's global edge network, which can reduce latency versus centralized VPN concentrators
Consolidates ZTNA, secure web gateway, browser isolation, CASB, and DLP into one platform with a single dashboard
No VPN hardware appliances required, with outbound-only connectors for exposing internal resources
Broad support for identity providers, letting existing SSO and group structures drive access policy
cons: The deepest CASB and advanced DLP capabilities are generally tied to Enterprise contracts rather than the self-serve plans
Effective policy design requires networking and security expertise; misconfiguration can lock out legitimate users
Enterprise pricing is not published and requires a sales conversation to get a quote
Migrating from an established VPN or a competing SASE platform involves real planning and cutover work
Self-serve plan support relies mainly on documentation and community resources rather than dedicated account support

faqs.question: What is Cloudflare Zero Trust?
faqs.answer: Cloudflare Zero Trust is Cloudflare's security suite for identity- and context-based access control, combining Zero Trust Network Access (Access), a secure web gateway (Gateway), browser isolation, CASB, and DLP under one platform called Cloudflare One.

faqs.question: Does Cloudflare Zero Trust replace a VPN?
faqs.answer: Yes, that is one of its core use cases. Instead of connecting a device to the entire corporate network, Cloudflare Access grants authenticated users access to individual applications, which Cloudflare positions as a safer and more scalable alternative to traditional VPNs.

faqs.question: Is there a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cloudflare offers a free tier covering up to 50 users with core ZTNA and secure web gateway functionality, limited DLP, and short log retention. Paid plans remove the user cap and add longer log retention and uptime SLAs.

faqs.question: What identity providers does it support?
faqs.answer: Cloudflare Zero Trust integrates with common identity providers including Okta, Microsoft Entra ID (Azure AD), Google Workspace, and other SAML/OIDC-compatible systems, so existing user and group structures can be reused for access policy.

faqs.question: How is Cloudflare Zero Trust different from Cloudflare's CDN and WAF products?
faqs.answer: Cloudflare's CDN and WAF products protect and accelerate public-facing websites and APIs. Cloudflare Zero Trust is a separate product line focused on securing how employees, contractors, and devices access internal applications, SaaS tools, and the internet, replacing VPNs and traditional perimeter security.

use_cases.title: VPN replacement for remote and hybrid teams
use_cases.description: Replace a traditional client VPN with per-application, identity-based access so remote and hybrid employees reach internal tools without being placed on the full corporate network.
use_cases.audience: IT and network security teams at mid-size to large organizations

use_cases.title: Secure third-party and contractor access
use_cases.description: Grant contractors, vendors, and partners scoped access to specific internal applications with full audit logging, without issuing broader VPN credentials.

use_cases.title: Web filtering and malware protection
use_cases.description: Apply DNS and HTTP-layer filtering, malware scanning, and acceptable-use policies to outbound internet traffic across managed devices.
use_cases.audience: IT administrators securing corporate internet access

use_cases.title: SaaS security posture and data protection
use_cases.description: Use CASB and DLP to find SaaS misconfigurations and prevent sensitive data such as customer records or credentials from leaving the organization.
use_cases.audience: Compliance and security teams in regulated industries

features: 24-hour DNS, HTTP, and network event log retention
Access to Browser Isolation and CASB as add-ons

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Compare Cloudflare Zero Trust pricing, features, pros and cons: ZTNA, secure web gateway, CASB, DLP, and browser isolation for VPN replacement.

enrichment.short_description: Clover is an all-in-one point-of-sale and payment processing platform with hardware and software for retail, restaurant, and service businesses.
enrichment.starting_price: Software plans advertised from around $14.95/month; final pricing (hardware, processing rates, contract terms) is set by the reseller or partner bank and requires a custom quote

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Clover is a point-of-sale (POS) and payment processing platform built for small and mid-sized businesses, combining proprietary hardware with cloud-based software. It was launched around 2010, later acquired by First Data, and is now owned and operated by Fiserv, one of the largest payment technology companies in the world. Rather than being sold as a single app, Clover is offered as a family of devices — including the countertop Clover Station, the compact Clover Mini, the handheld Clover Flex, and the mobile Clover Go card reader — paired with a cloud dashboard and mobile management app.

The software side covers the core operational needs of a physical storefront: sales and checkout, inventory tracking, employee scheduling and timeclocks, customer records, and reporting and analytics accessible from a web browser or the Clover mobile app. A distinguishing feature is the Clover App Market, an ecosystem of third-party and first-party apps that extend the base system with functions like loyalty programs, online ordering, accounting sync, and advanced reporting, similar in concept to a smartphone app store.

Clover is typically not purchased directly the way most SaaS products are. It is most often sold and supported through banks, credit unions, and independent sales organizations that act as resellers, which means pricing, contract terms, and even available hardware bundles can vary depending on who a merchant signs up through.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Clover Is For
paragraphs: Clover is aimed at brick-and-mortar businesses that need a combined hardware-and-software checkout solution rather than a pure software tool. Retail shops, cafes, quick-service restaurants, and full-service restaurants are the most common fit, since Clover's hardware lineup covers countertop registers, kitchen-facing handheld devices, and mobile card readers for tableside or line-busting service.

It also suits service-based businesses — salons, repair shops, and similar operations — that want appointment-adjacent invoicing, customer profiles, and simple inventory alongside payment acceptance. Businesses that already bank with an institution offering Clover as its branded POS solution, or that want a single vendor relationship for both hardware and payment processing, tend to find the setup convenient.

Merchants who want to extend the base system through add-on apps (loyalty, online ordering, accounting integrations) rather than building custom software are also well served, since the App Market is designed for exactly that kind of modular customization.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Businesses that want fully transparent, uniform pricing published on a single page should be cautious: because Clover is largely distributed through resellers and partner banks, quoted processing rates, monthly software fees, and contract lengths can differ significantly from one sales channel to another, and getting a final number typically requires a sales conversation.

Merchants who prioritize processor portability may also want to look elsewhere. Clover hardware and the merchant account behind it are tied to Fiserv's processing infrastructure, so switching payment processors later can mean replacing hardware or renegotiating with a new reseller, unlike processor-agnostic POS software.

Finally, purely online or e-commerce-only sellers with no physical checkout, or very early-stage businesses that want to avoid any hardware purchase or long-term agreement, are generally better matched to lighter-weight or hardware-free payment tools than to a countertop POS platform like Clover.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: At its core, Clover provides point-of-sale checkout across multiple hardware form factors, inventory management with item-level tracking, employee management with role permissions and timeclock functions, and built-in reporting and analytics viewable from a web dashboard or the Clover mobile app.

Beyond the base register functions, Clover supports customer engagement tools such as receipts capture for loyalty and marketing, online ordering for restaurants, invoicing for service businesses, and offline mode so transactions can still be processed during connectivity outages. The Clover App Market lets merchants add capabilities like advanced accounting sync, gift cards, and industry-specific tools without custom development.

For businesses with existing back-office software, Clover integrates with common accounting tools such as QuickBooks, and its developer platform exposes APIs that partners and independent developers use to build additional integrations and vertical-specific apps sold through the App Market.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Clover Experience
paragraphs: Day to day, using Clover feels closest to operating a modern tablet-based register: a touchscreen interface for ringing up sales, processing cards (chip, tap, and swipe), and managing tickets, paired with a companion web dashboard for owners to review sales, inventory, and staff activity remotely.

Onboarding is usually handled by the reseller or bank that sold the system, which can mean a more guided setup than self-serve SaaS products, but also means the support experience — response times, account changes, rate negotiations — depends heavily on which partner a merchant signed up through rather than on Fiserv/Clover directly.

Overall, merchants generally describe the hardware as polished and the day-to-day checkout flow as straightforward, with the more variable part of the experience being the sales and contracting process that happens before the system is ever turned on.

features.title: Point-of-sale checkout
features.description: Touchscreen register software running on Clover's own hardware line, supporting chip, tap, and swipe payments.

features.title: Inventory management
features.description: Track items, categories, stock levels, and variants directly from the POS or the web dashboard.

features.title: Employee management
features.description: Set staff permissions, schedules, and timeclock tracking tied to individual logins.

features.title: Reporting & analytics
features.description: View sales trends, item performance, and labor reports from a browser or the Clover mobile app.

features.title: Clover App Market
features.description: A marketplace of first- and third-party apps that add loyalty programs, online ordering, gift cards, and more.

features.title: Online ordering
features.description: Restaurant-focused ordering tools that connect incoming online orders to the in-store POS.

features.title: Multiple hardware form factors
features.description: Countertop (Station), compact (Mini), handheld (Flex), and mobile (Go) devices for different service styles.

features.title: Offline mode
features.description: Continue accepting payments during internet outages, with transactions syncing once connectivity returns.

features.title: Accounting integrations
features.description: Sync sales data with accounting software such as QuickBooks to reduce manual bookkeeping.

features.title: Developer API
features.description: APIs used by Clover partners and developers to build custom integrations and App Market listings.

pros: Wide range of purpose-built hardware for different business types (countertop, handheld, mobile)
Combines payments and POS operations (inventory, staff, reporting) in one connected system
Extensible through the Clover App Market instead of requiring custom development
Backed by Fiserv, a large, established payment processing company
Widely available through many banks and credit unions, making local support and onboarding easier for some merchants
cons: Pricing and contract terms are set by resellers/partner banks, making costs inconsistent and hard to compare upfront
Hardware and merchant accounts are tied to Fiserv's processing, limiting easy switching to another processor later
Some useful capabilities require paid App Market add-ons rather than being included by default
Support quality can vary depending on which reseller or bank the account was opened through
Long-term contracts and early termination fees are common in this space and can apply depending on the reseller

faqs.question: What is Clover used for?
faqs.answer: Clover is a point-of-sale and payment processing platform used by retail stores, restaurants, and service businesses to accept payments, manage inventory, track employees, and view sales reporting from one connected system of hardware and software.

faqs.question: Who owns Clover?
faqs.answer: Clover is owned and operated by Fiserv, a large payment technology company, after Fiserv's acquisition of First Data, which had previously acquired Clover.

faqs.question: How much does Clover cost?
faqs.answer: Clover's total cost depends on the hardware bundle, software plan, and payment processing rate, which are typically set by the bank or reseller that sells the account. Software plans have been advertised starting around $14.95 per month, with hardware and processing fees charged separately, so getting an exact quote usually requires contacting a sales representative.

faqs.question: Can I use Clover without a long-term contract?
faqs.answer: Contract terms vary by reseller and partner bank rather than being set uniformly by Clover itself; some offer month-to-month options while others use multi-year agreements, so merchants should review the specific agreement offered by their sales channel.

faqs.question: Does Clover work for restaurants as well as retail?
faqs.answer: Yes. Clover offers hardware and software configurations aimed at both retail checkout and restaurant service, including handheld devices for tableside ordering and online ordering tools for quick-service and full-service restaurants.

use_cases.title: Retail store checkout
use_cases.description: Running item-level inventory, barcode scanning, and daily sales reporting from a countertop or mini POS device.
use_cases.audience: Small to mid-sized retail shop owners

use_cases.title: Restaurant table and counter service
use_cases.description: Managing table-side ordering, kitchen tickets, and online orders alongside in-store checkout.

use_cases.title: Mobile and on-the-go payments
use_cases.description: Accepting card payments at markets, food trucks, or pop-up events using handheld or mobile card-reader hardware.

use_cases.title: Service business invoicing and payments
use_cases.description: Taking payments and issuing invoices for appointment-based or project-based services, with basic customer records.
use_cases.audience: Salons, repair shops, and similar service providers

features: Add-on apps available via App Market

enrichment.seo_meta_description: See what Clover POS offers: hardware, features, pricing, pros and cons, and who it's best suited for before you buy.

enrichment.short_description: Distributed SQL database built for resilience, horizontal scaling, and multi-region consistency, wire-compatible with PostgreSQL.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Serverless free tier); usage-based pricing beyond that

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: CockroachDB is a distributed SQL database built by Cockroach Labs, designed to survive failures ranging from a single disk crash to the loss of an entire data center or cloud region. Named after the cockroach's reputation for being nearly impossible to kill, the database replicates and distributes data automatically across nodes so that a cluster keeps serving reads and writes even when parts of the infrastructure go down. It exposes a PostgreSQL-compatible wire protocol and SQL dialect, which lets teams connect with familiar drivers, ORMs, and tools while gaining a fundamentally different storage and replication architecture underneath.

Under the hood, CockroachDB stores data in a distributed, replicated key-value layer and uses the Raft consensus protocol to keep replicas in sync, along with serializable isolation to guarantee strong transactional consistency even across geographically distributed nodes. This lets it behave, from an application's point of view, like a single logical database, while physically the data lives on many machines and potentially many regions. The product is available as a free, open-source, self-hosted core; as a fully managed CockroachDB Cloud (Serverless and Dedicated); and as an enterprise-oriented Advanced tier with additional operational and compliance features.

CockroachDB has become a common choice for teams that have outgrown a single-node relational database but don't want to give up SQL and strong consistency for a NoSQL or eventually-consistent system. It targets the middle ground between traditional databases like PostgreSQL or MySQL and horizontally scalable NoSQL stores, aiming to deliver the scalability and fault tolerance of the latter with the transactional guarantees and query language of the former.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who CockroachDB Is For
paragraphs: CockroachDB is best suited to engineering teams building applications that must stay online through infrastructure failures and that are outgrowing the capacity of a single-node relational database. This includes SaaS companies serving customers across multiple geographic regions, where low-latency reads and writes close to users matter, and organizations in regulated industries like finance, insurance, and healthcare that need strong consistency, auditability, and data residency controls alongside high availability.

It also appeals to platform and infrastructure teams running on Kubernetes or across multiple cloud providers who want a database that isn't tied to a single vendor's managed offering, since CockroachDB can be self-hosted anywhere or consumed as a managed cloud service. Teams already comfortable with PostgreSQL are natural adopters, since much of their existing SQL knowledge, drivers, and tooling carry over with relatively little friction.

Startups building for global scale from day one, and larger enterprises consolidating many regional databases into a single resilient cluster, both show up frequently among CockroachDB's user base, as do companies whose growth or compliance requirements make manual sharding of a traditional database increasingly painful to maintain.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: CockroachDB is not the right fit for small applications, side projects, or early-stage products where a single-node Postgres or MySQL instance already comfortably handles the load. The operational sophistication that gives CockroachDB its resilience — distributed consensus, replication, rebalancing — brings complexity and infrastructure cost that is unnecessary if you don't need multi-node fault tolerance or geographic distribution.

Teams with heavy reliance on Postgres-specific extensions, stored procedures, or advanced features not covered by CockroachDB's compatibility layer may run into friction, since compatibility is close but not total. Similarly, workloads that are extremely latency-sensitive on single-row point lookups at very high throughput, or that depend on niche database extensions and ecosystem tooling built specifically around vanilla PostgreSQL or MySQL, may be better served by those databases directly.

Organizations without in-house expertise (or budget for managed services) to operate a distributed system should also weigh the tradeoffs carefully; while CockroachDB Cloud removes much of that operational burden, self-hosting a distributed database still requires more monitoring and tuning discipline than a simple managed single-node database.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: CockroachDB's core capabilities center on distributed resilience and consistency. Data is automatically sharded into ranges and replicated across nodes using the Raft consensus algorithm, so the cluster continues operating and serving strongly consistent reads and writes even if individual nodes, availability zones, or entire regions fail. Multi-region table configurations (such as regional-by-row and global tables) let teams tune the tradeoff between latency and survivability on a per-table basis.

The database supports full ACID transactions with serializable isolation, the strongest level of consistency, and speaks the PostgreSQL wire protocol, so existing Postgres drivers, ORMs, and BI tools largely work without modification. Online, zero-downtime schema changes let teams alter tables in production without locking traffic, and built-in changefeeds (change data capture) allow streaming data changes to systems like Kafka or cloud storage for downstream processing.

Operationally, CockroachDB automatically rebalances data as nodes are added or removed, self-heals after failures without manual intervention, and can run identically on-premises, in Kubernetes, or across multiple public clouds — giving teams a consistent database layer regardless of where workloads are deployed.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Developer Experience
paragraphs: For developers, working with CockroachDB feels close to working with PostgreSQL: standard SQL syntax, familiar client libraries, and support for common ORMs mean most teams can get a basic application running quickly without learning a new query language. The CockroachDB Cloud console adds a layer of visibility into cluster health, query performance, and index usage, along with tools like the built-in SQL editor and insights panels that help identify slow or expensive queries.

The learning curve appears mostly around distributed-systems concepts rather than SQL itself — understanding range replication, locality settings for multi-region tables, and how transaction contention and retries behave differently in a distributed system compared to a single-node database. Cockroach Labs provides fairly extensive documentation, sample schemas, and migration guides aimed at easing this transition for teams coming from Postgres or MySQL.

Overall, teams generally describe the day-to-day experience as familiar for standard CRUD and transactional workloads, with the real payoff showing up under failure conditions and at scale — when nodes go down, regions become unreachable, or traffic grows well past what a single database instance could handle, and the cluster keeps serving without manual failover work.

features.title: Distributed SQL architecture
features.description: Automatically shards and replicates data across nodes while presenting a single logical SQL database to applications.

features.title: PostgreSQL wire compatibility
features.description: Supports the PostgreSQL wire protocol and much of its SQL dialect, so existing drivers, ORMs, and tools work with minimal changes.

features.title: Multi-region survivability
features.description: Configurable regional and global table settings let teams tune data placement for low latency or maximum fault tolerance across regions.

features.title: Serializable ACID transactions
features.description: Provides the strongest level of transactional isolation across a distributed cluster, avoiding many classes of data anomalies.

features.title: Automatic failover and self-healing
features.description: Detects node, zone, or region failures and automatically routes around them without manual intervention.

features.title: Online schema changes
features.description: Alter tables and indexes without taking the database offline or locking production traffic.

features.title: Automatic rebalancing
features.description: Redistributes data automatically as nodes are added or removed, keeping the cluster balanced under changing load.

features.title: Changefeeds (CDC)
features.description: Streams row-level data changes to external systems such as Kafka or cloud storage for downstream processing and analytics.

features.title: Cloud- and platform-agnostic deployment
features.description: Runs self-hosted on-premises, in Kubernetes, or across multiple public clouds, as well as fully managed via CockroachDB Cloud.

features.title: Horizontal scalability
features.description: Scales out by adding nodes rather than requiring larger single-machine hardware, supporting growth without re-architecting.

pros: Strong resilience — clusters keep operating through node, zone, or even region failures
PostgreSQL wire compatibility reduces the learning curve and eases migration from Postgres
Multi-region and multi-cloud deployment flexibility, including a fully managed cloud option
Full ACID transactions with serializable isolation, unusual for a horizontally scalable database
Online schema changes and automatic rebalancing reduce operational maintenance burden
cons: Distributed-systems concepts (ranges, locality, contention/retries) add a learning curve beyond plain SQL
Not a drop-in replacement for every Postgres extension or stored-procedure-heavy workload
Running a self-hosted cluster requires more operational sophistication than a single-node database
Cost and infrastructure overhead can be unnecessary for small applications that don't need distribution
Cross-region transactions can add latency compared to a single-region database by design of the consistency model

faqs.question: Is CockroachDB open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, CockroachDB's core database is source-available and free to self-host, alongside paid managed offerings (CockroachDB Cloud Serverless, Dedicated, and Advanced) for teams that want a hosted service.

faqs.question: Is CockroachDB compatible with PostgreSQL?
faqs.answer: CockroachDB implements the PostgreSQL wire protocol and much of its SQL syntax, so many existing Postgres drivers, ORMs, and tools work with little or no modification, though not every Postgres-specific feature is supported.

faqs.question: Can I try CockroachDB for free?
faqs.answer: Yes, CockroachDB Cloud offers a free Serverless tier with a monthly allowance, and the self-hosted core database is free and open source to run on your own infrastructure.

faqs.question: What makes CockroachDB different from a traditional relational database?
faqs.answer: Instead of running on a single machine, CockroachDB automatically distributes and replicates data across multiple nodes and regions using consensus-based replication, allowing it to survive failures and scale horizontally while still offering SQL and ACID transactions.

faqs.question: Who created CockroachDB?
faqs.answer: CockroachDB is developed by Cockroach Labs, a company founded in 2015 by former Google engineers and headquartered in New York City.

use_cases.title: Global, multi-region SaaS applications
use_cases.description: Serve customers in different geographic regions with low-latency reads and writes by placing data close to users while maintaining a single consistent database.

use_cases.title: Financial services and regulated workloads
use_cases.description: Meet strong consistency, auditability, and data-residency requirements using serializable transactions and configurable regional data placement.

use_cases.title: High-availability e-commerce and consumer platforms
use_cases.description: Keep checkout, inventory, and order systems online during traffic spikes or infrastructure failures without manual failover procedures.

use_cases.title: Multi-cloud and Kubernetes-native infrastructure
use_cases.description: Run a single consistent database layer across multiple cloud providers or on-premises Kubernetes clusters to avoid vendor lock-in.

features: Full core SQL and distributed features
Pay only for what you use beyond the free allowance
Advanced backup, DR, and audit capabilities

enrichment.short_description: Construction management software for custom home builders and remodelers to manage budgets, schedules, selections, and client communication.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: CoConstruct is construction management software built specifically for custom home builders, remodelers, and specialty residential contractors. Founded by Donny Wyatt after a frustrating experience building his own home, the platform grew over roughly two decades into one of the more established names in residential construction software, centering its workflow around client-facing selections, specs, budgets, schedules, and project communication rather than the broader commercial job-costing tools built for general contractors.

The product's whole premise was to replace the scattered emails, phone calls, and spreadsheets that typically accompany a custom build with one shared system that both the builder and the homeowner log into. That client-in-the-loop design, rather than a purely internal project-management tool, is what differentiated CoConstruct from generic construction software for most of its history.

In February 2021, CoConstruct was acquired by Buildertrend, with both companies operating under parent company ECI Software Solutions. Since the merger, CoConstruct has effectively moved into maintenance mode: it continues to serve existing customers, but ECI has been steadily consolidating its residential construction portfolio onto the Buildertrend platform, with a formal migration path and cutoff dates for new projects. Anyone evaluating CoConstruct today is really evaluating a legacy product with a defined transition plan, not an actively growing platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who CoConstruct Is For
paragraphs: CoConstruct was built for small-to-midsize custom home builders and remodeling companies that manage a handful of concurrent, high-touch client projects rather than a large volume of low-touch ones. Its selections and specs modules, in particular, were designed around the reality of custom construction, where a homeowner needs to review dozens of finish choices, allowances, and change orders over the life of a build, and the builder needs a single system to keep that dialogue, and the associated cost impact, organized.

Remodelers who juggle several active jobs with different clients, timelines, and finish selections at once are a similarly natural fit, since the platform's budgeting-against-allowances approach was designed for exactly that kind of variability. Design-build firms that sell the client experience as much as the construction itself also tend to value the polished, homeowner-facing portal.

Existing CoConstruct customers who are already comfortable with the interface, have integrated it with their accounting workflow, and don't want to disrupt active projects mid-build are the most natural fit for continuing to use it in the near term. It also suits builders who value a mature, full-featured tool over a newer, leaner one, since CoConstruct's feature set was refined over many years of direct feedback from residential trades before development slowed following the acquisition.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Builders and remodelers who are not already on CoConstruct should generally look elsewhere. The product is in maintenance mode, is not being actively marketed to new customers, and its own migration materials point prospective and existing users toward Buildertrend, with new-project creation inside CoConstruct set to end and a formal migration deadline for remaining accounts. Starting fresh on a platform that's being wound down creates unnecessary switching risk and wasted onboarding effort.

Commercial general contractors and companies whose work is primarily bid-and-build rather than client-facing custom construction are also a poor fit, since CoConstruct's tools are oriented around homeowner communication and selections rather than heavy-duty commercial job costing, prevailing-wage compliance, or large subcontractor networks.

Teams that need active, ongoing product investment, frequent new features, or up-to-date third-party integrations should weigh that against a platform whose vendor has openly been directing engineering resources toward its sister product instead. Builders prioritizing long-term platform stability are generally better served by evaluating Buildertrend directly, or a newer competitor, rather than committing to CoConstruct.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: CoConstruct's core strength is its client-facing communication layer: a shared online portal where homeowners can view schedules, approve selections, sign change orders, and message the builder without relying on email threads or phone calls. Layered on top of that is a specs and selections system that tracks allowances against actual costs, so a builder can show a client the budget impact of upgrading a fixture or finish in real time.

Budgeting and estimating tools let builders build cost catalogs, generate proposals, and track budget-to-actual variance by cost code, while a project schedule module, with subcontractor-facing views, coordinates trade sequencing across the life of a build. Change order management ties directly into the budget and client portal so approvals and cost adjustments stay in sync automatically rather than being re-entered by hand.

Document and photo storage keeps plans, permits, and job-site photos attached to the relevant project, with markup and annotation tools for reviewing drawings with clients or trades. Time tracking, an internal to-do and task system, and a warranty module for handling post-closing service requests round out the back-office side, while companion iOS and Android apps give builders and site supervisors field access to schedules, photos, and messages from the job site.

long_form_blocks.heading: The CoConstruct Experience
paragraphs: Day to day, using CoConstruct feels like managing a construction project through a single shared workspace with the client rather than through scattered emails, spreadsheets, and phone calls. Builders who adopted it in its more actively developed years generally describe the learning curve as moderate, given how much ground the software tries to cover, but describe the payoff as fewer client miscommunications, cleaner audit trails on selections, and fewer disputes over change-order costs at closeout.

Since the Buildertrend acquisition, the experience has shifted for many long-time customers: independent reviews and user reports commonly note that feature updates have slowed considerably, support responsiveness has declined from its earlier reputation, and the product's own in-app messaging now actively nudges customers toward migrating to Buildertrend rather than introducing new capabilities within CoConstruct itself.

For a directory evaluation today, the honest characterization is a capable, purpose-built tool for its era, one that built genuine loyalty among custom builders for its client communication model, but that current and prospective users should approach with its transition trajectory clearly in mind rather than as a long-term platform bet.

features.description: A shared online workspace where homeowners can view project updates, message the builder, and stay in the loop without relying on email or phone calls.

features.title: Budgeting & Estimating
features.description: Cost catalogs, proposals, and budget-to-actual tracking by cost code, built around the way custom builders and remodelers price jobs.

features.title: Scheduling
features.description: Project schedules with subcontractor-facing views to coordinate trade sequencing and keep everyone aligned on timing.

features.title: Change Order Management
features.description: Digital change orders with e-signatures that automatically flow through to the budget and the client portal.

features.title: Documents, Photos & Plan Markup
features.description: Centralized storage for plans, permits, and job-site photos, with annotation tools for reviewing drawings with clients or trades.

features.title: Time Tracking
features.description: Crew and subcontractor time entry tied back to jobs and cost codes for labor cost tracking.

features.title: Warranty Management
features.description: A dedicated module for logging, assigning, and closing out post-closing warranty and service requests.

features.title: Mobile Apps
features.description: iOS and Android companion apps that give field staff access to schedules, photos, messages, and task lists on the job site.

pros: Purpose-built for the custom home building and remodeling workflow, including selections, allowances, and client-facing change orders
Strong client communication portal that reduces email and phone back-and-forth with homeowners
Mature, deeply developed feature set refined over roughly two decades serving residential builders
Budgeting tools tailored to construction cost codes rather than generic project accounting
A defined, supported migration path to Buildertrend for customers who need to transition
cons: In maintenance mode since the 2021 acquisition, with little to no new feature development
Not open to meaningful new customer growth; the vendor is actively directing prospects to Buildertrend instead
Pricing is no longer published on the company's site and requires contacting sales or support
User reports describe slower support response times compared to CoConstruct's earlier, independent years
A defined cutoff for creating new projects means long-term commitment to the platform itself is not realistic

faqs.question: Is CoConstruct still available to new customers?
faqs.answer: CoConstruct is in maintenance mode and is not being actively marketed to new customers. Parent company ECI Software Solutions is directing prospective and existing customers toward Buildertrend, its sister platform, as the long-term product.

faqs.question: What happens to my data if I'm an existing CoConstruct customer?
faqs.answer: According to CoConstruct's own migration guidance, historical project data is retained and accessible indefinitely, and a dedicated migration team assists existing customers moving their projects into Buildertrend.

faqs.question: What's the difference between CoConstruct and Buildertrend?
faqs.answer: Both are residential construction management platforms with overlapping features (budgeting, scheduling, client communication, selections). Buildertrend acquired CoConstruct in 2021, and both now operate under parent company ECI Software Solutions, with Buildertrend positioned as the platform going forward.

faqs.question: How much does CoConstruct cost?
faqs.answer: CoConstruct no longer publishes pricing directly on its website; prospective and existing customers are directed to contact sales or support for current terms.

faqs.question: What types of companies typically use CoConstruct?
faqs.answer: Custom home builders, remodelers, and specialty residential contractors who manage a smaller number of high-touch, client-facing projects rather than high-volume production building or commercial work.

use_cases.title: Managing client selections and allowances
use_cases.description: Tracking dozens of finish and material choices per project against allowance budgets, and showing homeowners the cost impact of each decision in real time.

use_cases.title: Coordinating multi-project remodels
use_cases.description: Running several concurrent remodeling jobs with different clients, timelines, and finish selections from a single system.

use_cases.title: Managing change orders and budgets together
use_cases.description: Keeping change order approvals, e-signatures, and budget adjustments in sync so cost overruns are caught and communicated before closeout.

use_cases.title: Transitioning off a sunsetting platform
use_cases.description: Using CoConstruct's built-in migration tools and support to move active and historical projects onto Buildertrend ahead of the new-project cutoff.

Not currently published or sold on coconstruct.com

CoConstruct is in maintenance mode under parent company ECI Software Solutions
