enrichment.short_description: AI coding assistant offering autocomplete, in-IDE chat, and the agentic Windsurf IDE, built by Exafunction and now owned by Cognition AI.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Codeium is an AI-powered coding assistant originally built by Exafunction, a company founded in 2021 by MIT classmates Varun Mohan and Douglas Chen in Mountain View, California. Both had backgrounds in performance-critical infrastructure work, Mohan on deep learning systems for autonomous vehicles at Nuro and Chen on developer tooling for VR headsets at Meta, before turning their GPU-optimization startup into a developer-productivity company. The product was rebranded as Codeium in 2022 and quickly gained attention for offering fast, free in-editor autocomplete and chat to individual programmers.

Over time, the company built Windsurf, a standalone agentic coding IDE forked from Visual Studio Code and centered on an AI agent called Cascade, capable of planning and executing multi-file changes, running terminal commands, and reasoning across an entire codebase. Windsurf became the company's flagship product through 2024 and 2025, and the business itself came to be widely known as Windsurf, while 'Codeium' continued on as the name for its lighter-weight editor extensions.

In July 2025, following a licensing arrangement that moved part of the founding team, including CEO Varun Mohan, to Google DeepMind, Cognition AI, maker of the autonomous software engineer Devin, acquired the remaining Windsurf business, including its product, brand, and customer base. Both the Codeium extensions and the Windsurf IDE have continued to be developed and supported under Cognition's ownership.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Codeium Is For
paragraphs: Individual developers, students, and hobbyists who want free, low-latency AI autocomplete and chat without leaving their existing editor are a core audience for Codeium; the free individual plan is generous enough for everyday personal projects and learning to code.

Small teams and startups that want one vendor covering both lightweight in-editor assistance, through the Codeium extensions, and a more autonomous, agentic development environment, through the Windsurf IDE, can standardize on the tool without juggling separate subscriptions or context.

Security-conscious or regulated engineering organizations that need private, self-hosted, or VPC deployments of an AI coding assistant are also well served, since the enterprise tier is built around on-prem and controlled-network deployment options.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Developers who prioritize a single, stable long-term brand may find the Codeium-to-Windsurf transition and the 2025 change of ownership to Cognition AI a source of uncertainty, and should confirm current product direction and support commitments before standardizing a whole team on it.

Anyone who cannot send code context to a hosted inference service, and who isn't ready to pay for the enterprise self-hosted tier, should look elsewhere, since the free and Pro plans rely on cloud-based models rather than fully local inference.

Teams already deeply invested in GitHub Copilot's tight GitHub and GitHub Actions integration, or that specifically want Cursor's editor-first workflow and community, may not find enough differentiation in Codeium's extensions to justify switching tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: At its core, Codeium provides multi-line, context-aware autocomplete across dozens of programming languages, plus an in-IDE chat panel that can explain code, generate unit tests, and refactor snippets using the surrounding repository as context rather than just the open file.

These capabilities ship as extensions for a wide range of editors, including VS Code, JetBrains IDEs such as IntelliJ, PyCharm, and WebStorm, Visual Studio, Vim/Neovim, Emacs, Eclipse, and notebook environments like Jupyter, Colab, and Databricks, so teams are not forced onto a single editor to get AI assistance.

On top of the extensions, the Windsurf IDE adds an agentic layer built around Cascade, which can edit multiple files, propose and run terminal commands, and carry out longer development tasks end to end, while enterprise customers additionally get self-hosted deployment, centralized admin controls, and usage analytics.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Windsurf Experience
paragraphs: Windsurf is the company's standalone IDE, built as a fork of Visual Studio Code and designed around collaborative 'flows' where the developer and the Cascade agent work turn by turn, rather than the developer simply accepting isolated autocomplete suggestions.

Cascade can read and edit across multiple files, propose and execute terminal commands, and use its own understanding of project structure to plan larger changes, which sets the experience apart from the simpler autocomplete-and-chat model of the original Codeium extensions.

Since Cognition's 2025 acquisition, Windsurf has continued to ship updates that draw on Cognition's own agentic engineering work, positioning it as one of the more actively developed agentic IDEs in a market that also includes Cursor and GitHub Copilot's agent mode.

features.title: AI code autocomplete
features.description: Multi-line, context-aware code suggestions across dozens of programming languages as you type.

features.title: In-IDE chat
features.description: A conversational panel for explaining code, answering questions, and generating snippets using repository context.

features.title: Cascade agent (Windsurf)
features.description: An autonomous agent in the Windsurf IDE that can plan and execute multi-file edits and run terminal commands.

features.title: Broad editor coverage
features.description: Extensions for VS Code, JetBrains IDEs, Visual Studio, Vim/Neovim, Emacs, and Eclipse, so teams aren't locked into one editor.

features.title: Notebook support
features.description: Integrations for Jupyter, Google Colab, and Databricks notebooks for data science and ML workflows.

features.title: Codebase-aware context
features.description: Retrieval across the wider repository to make suggestions and chat answers more relevant than single-file context alone.

features.title: Inline commands
features.description: In-editor commands to explain, refactor, document, or generate tests for selected code.

features.title: Free individual tier
features.description: A no-cost plan for solo developers and students with core autocomplete and chat functionality.

features.title: Self-hosted enterprise deployment
features.description: On-prem or VPC deployment options for organizations with strict data-residency or compliance requirements.

features.title: Admin and analytics controls
features.description: Centralized billing, usage analytics, and organization-wide settings for Teams and Enterprise customers.

pros: Generous free tier for individual developers and students
Extensions cover an unusually wide range of editors beyond VS Code
Fast, low-latency autocomplete suggestions
Windsurf's Cascade agent adds real multi-file, agentic editing on top of the base assistant
Enterprise self-hosted deployment options for regulated or security-conscious organizations
cons: The Codeium-to-Windsurf rebrand and 2025 change of ownership to Cognition AI has created some confusion about product direction and support
Free tier usage limits are more restrictive than paid plans, especially for chat and agentic features
The most advanced agentic capabilities are increasingly concentrated in the separate Windsurf IDE rather than the lightweight extensions
Cloud-based inference means code context is sent off-device unless an organization is on the self-hosted enterprise plan
Faces intense competition from Cursor and GitHub Copilot in a fast-moving market, which could affect long-term roadmap priorities

faqs.question: Is Codeium free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Codeium offers a free individual plan with core autocomplete and chat functionality in supported editors; paid Pro, Teams, and Enterprise plans add higher usage limits and collaboration features.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Codeium and Windsurf?
faqs.answer: Codeium was the original brand for the company's lightweight AI autocomplete and chat extensions. Windsurf is the company's standalone, agentic IDE built around the Cascade agent, and it became the company's primary product and eventual company name.

faqs.question: Who owns Codeium and Windsurf today?
faqs.answer: In July 2025, Cognition AI, the maker of the autonomous software engineer Devin, acquired the Windsurf business, including its product, brand, and customer base, after some of the founding team moved to Google DeepMind under a separate licensing arrangement.

faqs.question: Which code editors does Codeium support?
faqs.answer: Codeium extensions are available for VS Code, JetBrains IDEs (IntelliJ, PyCharm, WebStorm, and others), Visual Studio, Vim/Neovim, Emacs, Eclipse, and notebook environments such as Jupyter, Colab, and Databricks.

faqs.question: Does Codeium offer enterprise or self-hosted deployment?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Enterprise plan supports self-hosted or VPC deployment along with admin controls, usage analytics, and dedicated support for organizations with compliance or data-residency requirements.

use_cases.title: Daily solo development
use_cases.description: Using free autocomplete and chat directly inside an existing editor to speed up everyday coding tasks.

use_cases.title: Learning to code
use_cases.description: Getting free, context-aware explanations and suggestions while working through coursework or personal projects.

use_cases.title: AI-assisted team development
use_cases.description: Adopting shared autocomplete, chat, and the agentic Windsurf IDE across an engineering team with centralized billing and analytics.

use_cases.title: Compliance-sensitive engineering
use_cases.description: Running a self-hosted or VPC deployment of the assistant so code context never leaves an organization's controlled network.

features: Core AI code autocomplete in supported editors
Higher usage limits for chat and agentic features

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Codeium review: pricing, features, supported editors, and pros and cons of this AI coding assistant, now part of Cognition's Windsurf.

enrichment.short_description: No-code online form builder known for Excel-style calculations, conditional logic, and built-in payment collection.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Cognito Forms is an online form builder created by Cognito, LLC, a Columbia, South Carolina company founded in 2013 by Jamie Thomas and Jennifer Dellacroce. It was built to bridge the gap between simple survey tools and full custom applications, letting non-developers create forms that handle real business logic rather than just collecting text answers.

The platform's signature strength is its formula engine: users can build spreadsheet-style calculations, conditional show/hide logic, repeating sections and tables, and dynamic pricing directly into a form without writing code. On top of that, it layers native payment processing (Stripe, PayPal, Square, Authorize.Net), electronic signatures, save-and-resume, and multi-step approval workflows.

Cognito Forms operates on a freemium model, with a genuinely usable free tier and paid plans that unlock higher entry volumes, more users, and enterprise features like HIPAA compliance and single sign-on. The company was acquired by private equity firm Inverness Graham in 2025 but continues to operate under the Cognito Forms brand.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Cognito Forms Is For
paragraphs: Small businesses and solopreneurs are a natural fit, especially those that need quote calculators, order forms, or booking forms that do math on the fly (shipping costs, quantity discounts, tax) without hiring a developer or juggling spreadsheets after the fact.

Nonprofits and membership associations rely heavily on Cognito Forms for donation forms with integrated payment processing, event registration with capacity limits, volunteer sign-ups, and membership applications — use cases the product markets itself around directly.

Internal operations, HR, and finance teams also get real value from it: purchase requests, expense reimbursement, employee onboarding, and other processes that need e-signatures, file uploads, and routed multi-step approvals rather than a static PDF passed around by email.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Should Avoid It
paragraphs: Teams that prioritize a highly polished, conversational-style front end — the one-question-at-a-time, animation-heavy feel popularized by tools like Typeform — will likely find Cognito Forms more utilitarian in appearance. It's built for logic and function first, visual flair second.

Organizations with very high monthly submission volumes may find the entry-based pricing tiers add up faster than platforms with flat unlimited-response pricing, so it's worth mapping expected volume against the plan limits before committing.

Companies whose workflow already lives deeply inside a CRM like Salesforce or HubSpot may prefer that platform's native form tools for tighter data sync, rather than layering a separate standalone form builder on top, even one as capable as Cognito Forms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Main Features
paragraphs: At the core is conditional logic and calculations: fields can appear, hide, or auto-populate based on other answers, and formulas can reference multiple fields the way spreadsheet formulas reference cells, enabling live pricing, scoring, or eligibility checks inside the form itself.

Data-collection features go beyond single submissions, with repeating sections and tables for line items, file uploads, electronic signatures, save-and-resume for long forms, and a client portal where respondents can view or edit their own past entries.

Operationally, forms can trigger multi-step approval workflows, generate merged PDF or Word documents from submitted data, and connect outward through a native API, Zapier, and Microsoft Power Automate, alongside direct integrations with tools like Salesforce and Mailchimp.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Cognito Forms Experience
paragraphs: Building a form feels part spreadsheet, part form designer: the drag-and-drop editor is straightforward for basic fields, but unlocking the calculation engine and nested logic has a real learning curve, softened somewhat by a library of hundreds of pre-built templates to start from.

Once published, forms embed cleanly into existing websites and render responsively on mobile, while the entries dashboard provides views, filtering, and reporting on submitted data without needing to export to a separate tool for basic analysis.

Support is delivered through documentation, a community forum, and email or chat support on paid plans, and the overall experience positions Cognito Forms squarely between bare-bones tools like Google Forms and heavier database-application platforms — more powerful than the former, simpler to pick up than the latter.

features.title: Conditional logic
features.description: Show, hide, or require fields dynamically based on a respondent's previous answers.

features.title: Excel-style calculations
features.description: Build formulas that reference other fields to compute totals, pricing, scores, or eligibility in real time.

features.title: Integrated payments
features.description: Accept payments directly on a form through Stripe, PayPal, Square, or Authorize.Net without a separate checkout step.

features.title: Repeating sections and tables
features.description: Let respondents add multiple line items, such as order quantities or additional guests, within a single submission.

features.title: Electronic signatures
features.description: Collect legally binding e-signatures as part of a form submission for contracts, waivers, or approvals.

features.title: Workflow and approvals
features.description: Route submitted entries through multi-step approval chains for internal processes like purchase requests or onboarding.

features.title: Save and resume
features.description: Allow respondents to save a partially completed long form and return to finish it later.

features.title: Document generation
features.description: Automatically merge entry data into PDF or Word document templates for contracts, receipts, or reports.

features.title: Templates library
features.description: Start from a large collection of prebuilt form templates for common use cases across industries.

features.title: API and integrations
features.description: Connect forms to other systems through a native API, Zapier, Microsoft Power Automate, and direct integrations.

pros: Generous free-forever plan with unlimited forms, not just a time-limited trial
Calculation and conditional logic engine is unusually powerful for a form builder
Native payment collection covers multiple processors without extra add-ons
Built-in workflow and approval routing suits internal operations use cases
Large template library shortens setup for common form types
cons: Entry-based pricing can grow expensive for high-submission-volume organizations
User and guest limits on lower tiers require upgrading as teams grow
Advanced formulas and logic have a steeper learning curve than basic form builders
Visual design and branding options are less flashy than conversational-style competitors
HIPAA compliance and single sign-on are restricted to the top-tier Enterprise plan

faqs.question: Is Cognito Forms free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Cognito Forms offers a free Individual plan with unlimited forms, up to 100 entries per month, and access to core features like conditional logic and payments; paid plans raise entry limits, add users, and unlock features like e-signatures and workflows.

faqs.question: Does Cognito Forms support online payments?
faqs.answer: Yes, forms can collect payments directly through integrations with Stripe, PayPal, Square, and Authorize.Net, so respondents can pay while submitting a form without a separate checkout.

faqs.question: Is Cognito Forms HIPAA compliant?
faqs.answer: Cognito Forms offers HIPAA compliance with a signed Business Associate Agreement on its Team and Enterprise plans, which is relevant for healthcare organizations collecting patient information.

faqs.question: Do I need coding skills to use Cognito Forms?
faqs.answer: No, Cognito Forms is a no-code platform built around a visual drag-and-drop editor, though building advanced calculations and conditional logic does involve a learning curve similar to spreadsheet formulas.

faqs.question: What does Cognito Forms integrate with?
faqs.answer: Cognito Forms connects to other tools through a native API, Zapier, and Microsoft Power Automate, along with direct integrations such as Salesforce and Mailchimp.

use_cases.title: Nonprofit donation and event forms
use_cases.description: Collect donations with integrated payment processing and manage event registrations with capacity limits and automated confirmations.

use_cases.title: Small business order and quote forms
use_cases.description: Build forms that calculate pricing, taxes, and shipping automatically based on customer selections.

use_cases.title: Internal approval workflows
use_cases.description: Route purchase requests, expense reports, or onboarding paperwork through multi-step approval chains with e-signatures.

use_cases.title: Registration and application forms
use_cases.description: Manage volunteer sign-ups, membership applications, or program registrations with conditional logic and save-and-resume.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Compare Cognito Forms' free plan, pricing, calculation engine, payment integrations, and workflow features before you build your next form.
